title: Dropout Universality: Scaling Laws and Optimal Scheduling at the Edge-of-Chaos
abstract: We develop a mean-field theory of dropout as a perturbation of critical signal propagation at the edge of chaos. Dropout shifts the perfect-alignment fixed point, making the depth scale for information propagation finite even at critical initialization. We derive critical and crossover scaling laws for correlation decay and establish that smooth activations and kinked, ReLU-like activations constitute distinct universality classes, with different critical exponents and a universal two-parameter scaling collapse in detuning and dropout strength. The distinction traces to the analytic structure of the correlation map: smooth activations admit a Taylor expansion near perfect alignment, while kinked activations develop a branch point with universal non-analyticity. As a corollary, the framework yields saturated dropout profiles under fixed budget; a rank-flow tie-breaker then selects front-loaded schedules, substantially reducing held-out test loss at no extra computational cost, with accuracy gains as a consistent secondary effect. We test the predictions in MLPs and Vision Transformers and discuss CNN/ResNet extensions.
summary: The paper provides a theoretical framework to understand how dropout shifts the critical point of signal propagation in deep networks. It discovers that activation function smoothness determines the network's universality class and proves that applying dropout more heavily to early layers optimizes performance by preventing rank collapse.
main_problem: Understanding how dropout acts as a relevant perturbation that deforms the edge-of-chaos phase diagram and determining the optimal depth-dependent dropout schedule to prevent rank collapse.
main_result: Identified distinct universality classes for smooth vs. kinked activations and demonstrated that front-loading dropout (concentrating it in early layers) significantly improves generalization and prevents token diversity decay.
method: Mean-field theory using a Landau-style expansion and renormalization group-like analysis of signal propagation, supplemented by numerical simulations on MLPs and Vision Transformers.
model_or_system: Deep neural networks, specifically Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs), analyzed in the infinite-width/large-channel limit.
key_observables: Order parameter (m), correlation length (xi), correlation susceptibility (chi), effective rank (participation ratio), and test loss/accuracy.
important_parameters: Dropout strength (h), detuning from criticality (t), activation function smoothness, and network depth/width ratio.
main_assumptions: Assumes the large-width limit (L/N << 1) to control non-Gaussian corrections and i.i.d. Gaussian initialization.
figures_summary: Training curves for loss and accuracy under different schedules, visual comparisons of component-level ablations (Attention vs. MLP), and scaling collapse plots for different dropout strengths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dropout-induced deformation of the critical point, develops a mean-field theory for smooth and kinked activation universality classes, derives scaling laws and optimal scheduling via variational calculus, and validates these predictions through empirical tests on MLPs and ViTs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Critical scaling for smooth (tanh) and kinked (ReLU) activation functions, comparing tuning at zero dropout to tuning at the edge-of-chaos using a dropout field. The top row compares the different critical exponents at zero dropout and probes critical detuning decay, while the bottom row explores on critical networks with non-zero dropout. As the variables grow, higher-order effects become comparable and the linearized theory departs from the full recursion.

Figure 2. Two-parameter crossover and scaling collapse of the dropout-deformed equation of state for the smooth universality class (tanh). Plots obtained using MFT recursion relations. The curves collapse onto a universal function after rescaling by ˜t and ˜m. The kinked counterpart is shown in Fig. 4.

Figure 3. Magnitude of Hermite coefficients |an| for ReLU versus tanh. ReLU exhibits a slow (power-law) decay, reflecting multi-scale support across Hermite degrees, while tanh decays rapidly and concentrates most spectral mass in the lowest modes.
Figure 4. Kinked counterpart to Fig. 1, showing the corresponding universal scaling collapse.

Figure 5. Finite-width MLP training and test curves at fixed mean dropout budget ¯h (with 0 ≤hℓ≤hmax). We compare uniform dropout, linear ramps (increasing/decreasing with depth), and step schedules that concentrate dropout in either the first or last half of the network, together with the no-dropout baseline.
